q: What does "semiconservative" replication mean, and what are the two other models Meselson–Stahl tested?
a: Each daughter duplex = one conserved **parental** strand + one **new** strand. The rejected alternatives: **conservative** (one all-old duplex + one all-new) and **dispersive** (each strand a patchwork of old and new).

q: Who proved semiconservative replication, when, and by what method?
a: **Meselson & Stahl, 1958**, using **CsCl density-gradient ultracentrifugation** — grew *E. coli* in heavy ¹⁵N, switched to light ¹⁴N, and tracked DNA density each generation.

q: Who reported the first evidence of a single origin of replication in *E. coli*, and when?
a: **John Cairns, 1963** (autoradiography of the replicating circular chromosome → the "theta"/replication-bubble image).

q: In which direction do all DNA polymerases synthesize, and what do they add onto?
a: **5′→3′** — they add a new nucleotide's 5′-phosphate onto the free **3′-OH** of the growing strand. ("Follow the 3′ hydroxyl.") They read the template 3′→5′.

q: Why does DNA replication need an RNA primer, and what makes it?
a: DNA polymerase **cannot start de novo** — it can only extend a 3′-OH. **Primase** (DnaG) synthesizes a short **RNA** primer (~12–24 nt, contains uracil) that provides that first 3′-OH. RNA primers are later removed.

q: Describe *E. coli* oriC: size, base composition, and its two sequence elements.
a: **oriC** ≈ **245 bp**, **AT-rich**. Contains three **13-mers** (AT-rich tandem repeats — the meltable "DNA unwinding element") and four **9-mers** (**DnaA boxes**, consensus **TTATCCACA**).

q: What do DnaA, DnaB, and DnaC each do at oriC?
a: **DnaA** binds the **9-mers**, bends the DNA, and opens (melts) the AT-rich **13-mers** → open complex. **DnaC** loads **DnaB** (the **helicase**) onto the single strands; DnaB unwinds the duplex bidirectionally.

q: What does helicase do, and what does single-strand binding protein (SSB) do?
a: **Helicase (DnaB)** breaks hydrogen bonds to unwind the double helix ahead of the fork. **SSB** coats the exposed single strands to stop them re-annealing.

q: How many origins does a bacterial vs a eukaryotic chromosome use?
a: Bacterial (small, circular) = **one** origin (bidirectional). Eukaryotic (large, linear) = **many** — yeast ~400, *Drosophila* ~10,000, human up to ~50,000.

q: Leading vs lagging strand — how is each synthesized?
a: **Leading strand** = synthesized **continuously** toward the fork (one primer). **Lagging strand** = synthesized **discontinuously** away from the fork, as short **Okazaki fragments**, each needing its own RNA primer.

q: What are Okazaki fragments, who found them, and how long are they?
a: Short DNA segments of the **lagging strand** (discontinuous synthesis), detected by **Reiji Okazaki**. **E. coli: ~1000–2000 nt; eukaryotes: ~100–200 nt.**

q: What does DNA polymerase III do, and how many are in a replisome?
a: **DNA Pol III** = the principal DNA synthesizer (high processivity, 3′→5′ proofreading), starting at a primer's 3′-OH. **Two** Pol III per **replisome** (one leading, one lagging).

q: What two activities does DNA polymerase I use to deal with RNA primers?
a: Its **5′→3′ exonuclease** chews out RNA-primer nucleotides one by one, while its **5′→3′ polymerase** fills in DNA behind. (Pol I also has 3′→5′ proofreading + does DNA repair.)

q: What does DNA ligase do?
a: Forms a **phosphodiester bond** to seal the single-stranded **nick** between adjacent DNA fragments — joining **Okazaki fragments** into a continuous strand. Cofactor: in *E. coli* (bacteria) DNA ligase is **NAD⁺-dependent**; eukaryotic and T4-phage ligases use **ATP**.

q: What is the sliding clamp — its shape, and its function?
a: A ring (~**75 Å** across, ~**35 Å** hole) that **encircles double-stranded DNA** and tethers DNA Pol III to the template → dramatically boosts **processivity** (how far the polymerase stays on the track).

q: What is proofreading, which activity performs it, and how accurate is replication?
a: Removing a just-added wrong nucleotide via the **3′→5′ exonuclease** activity (step back, clip, retry). Error rate ≈ **1 in 10⁹** nucleotides in wild-type *E. coli*.

q: What is supercoiling, and what relieves it?
a: **Supercoiling** = torsional strain from unwinding the helix during replication. **Topoisomerases** (a.k.a. gyrases) cut one or both strands, let the DNA rotate/untwist, then rejoin it. (They have re-joining ability but are **not** DNA ligase.)

q: Name the three principal bacterial replication polymerases and their jobs (Table 7.3).
a: **DnaG** (primase) = RNA primer synthesis; **Pol I** = RNA-primer removal + proofreading + repair; **Pol III** = bulk DNA replication + proofreading. (Eukaryotes use Greek: α/primase, δ lagging, ε leading — **don't memorize the Greek ones except γ**, which returns in repair.)

q: What is the end-replication problem?
a: On a **linear** chromosome, when the terminal lagging-strand RNA primer is removed there's **no upstream 3′-OH to fill the gap** → a **3′ overhang** forms and the chromosome **shortens each replication cycle**.

q: What are telomeres — composition, function, and the vertebrate repeat?
a: Repetitive, non-coding DNA at linear chromosome ends (vertebrate repeat **5′-TTAGGG-3′**, ~6 bp; 2–20 kb at birth). Being repetitive/gene-free, they can be safely lost bit-by-bit each cycle, buffering the end-replication problem.

q: What kind of enzyme is telomerase (its two components and its activity)?
a: A **ribonucleoprotein** = **protein + an RNA template**. It's a **reverse transcriptase** — an **RNA-dependent DNA polymerase** (makes DNA off its own RNA template), encoded by the **TERT** gene. It extends the G-rich 3′ overhang.

q: Who discovered telomeres/telomerase, in what organism, and what prize followed?
a: **Elizabeth Blackburn & Carol Greider (1987)** in the ciliate *Tetrahymena*; with **Jack Szostak** they won the **2009 Nobel Prize** in Physiology or Medicine.

q: What is Shelterin + the T-loop, and what do they do?
a: **Shelterin** (a protein complex; components TRF/TPP1/POT1 — don't memorize the parts) folds the telomere into a **T-loop** (the end tucks back and base-pairs), **capping** it so double-strand-break repair can't fuse chromosome ends.

q: What is the Hayflick limit — who, when, and its cause?
a: **Leonard Hayflick, 1965** — cultured vertebrate cells divide only **~50–70 times** then stop (senescence) or die. Cause = progressive **telomere shortening**.

q: What is the relationship between telomerase and cancer?
a: Telomerase is **reactivated in ~80–90% of cancers**, letting cells escape the Hayflick limit and divide indefinitely (evading telomere-triggered death) — a hallmark of cancer's "immortality."

q: What is PCR, who invented it, and when?
a: **Polymerase Chain Reaction** — an in-vitro, automated DNA replication that exponentially amplifies a target sequence. Invented by **Kary Mullis, 1983**.

q: Name PCR's three cycle steps with their temperatures and what happens at each.
a: **Denature (~95 °C)** — heat melts the strands apart. **Anneal (~45–68 °C)** — primers hybridize to the template. **Extend (~72 °C)** — Taq polymerase builds new DNA from each primer's 3′-OH.

q: What is Taq polymerase, where is it from, and why was it chosen for PCR?
a: DNA polymerase from ***Thermus aquaticus*** (hot-spring bacterium; first collected by **Brock, Yellowstone, 1965**). Chosen because it's **thermostable** — it survives the ~95 °C denaturation step (~1000 bp/min; but lacks 3′→5′ proofreading).

q: How many copies does PCR make after n cycles, and roughly after 30?
a: **2ⁿ** (each cycle doubles the target). After 30 cycles ≈ **2³⁰ ≈ 1 billion** copies.

q: What is Sanger sequencing, who developed it and when, and what molecule terminates the chain?
a: **Sanger (chain-termination) sequencing** — **Frederick Sanger, 1977** (Maxam & Gilbert published a different method the same year). Chains terminate when a **dideoxynucleotide (ddNTP)** is incorporated.

q: Why does a ddNTP terminate a growing DNA strand?
a: A **dideoxynucleotide** lacks the **3′-OH** (it's deoxygenated at BOTH the 2′ and 3′ carbons). With no 3′-OH, no further phosphodiester bond can form → **chain termination**.

q: In modern (automated) Sanger sequencing, how is the sequence read out?
a: Each ddNTP base carries a different **fluorescent** color; fragments are separated by **capillary gel electrophoresis** and a detector reads the colored peaks as a **chromatogram**.

q: How does high-throughput / shotgun (Illumina) sequencing work in brief?
a: Break the genome into millions of tiny fragments; do **reversible** chain termination — add a base, image its color, chemically **restore the 3′-OH**, repeat — one base at a time, massively in parallel. A computer overlaps the reads into **contigs** → a **reference genome**.

q: How do the generation-by-generation bands distinguish the three replication models?
a: **Gen 1** separates **conservative** (which gives a pure-**light** band immediately) from **semiconservative/dispersive** (both give only an intermediate/**hybrid** band). **Gen 2** separates **semiconservative** (a discrete **light** band appears alongside constant hybrid) from **dispersive** (a single band that just drifts steadily lighter, never splitting).

q: In semiconservative replication, what happens to the hybrid band vs the light band across generations?
a: The **hybrid** (heavy–light) band stays a **constant amount** (2 duplexes — the original parental strands persist as hybrids); the **light** (light–light) band **grows** each cycle. So later gens show a constant hybrid + an ever-thickening light band.

q: Why must DNA replication start with an RNA primer instead of DNA?
a: DNA polymerase can only **extend** an existing 3′-OH — it can't initiate a strand from scratch. **RNA polymerase/primase CAN** start de novo, so a short RNA primer lays down the first 3′-OH for DNA pol to build on. (The RNA is later removed because it's chemically labile.)

q: Why is the lagging strand synthesized discontinuously (in Okazaki fragments)?
a: The two strands are **antiparallel**, but polymerase only builds **5′→3′**. On the strand running the "wrong" way relative to fork movement, synthesis must repeatedly restart (new primer + short fragment) as the fork exposes more template → discontinuous **Okazaki fragments**.

q: Mechanistically, how does DnaA binding the 9-mers open the origin?
a: **DnaA** binds the four **9-mer** boxes and oligomerizes, **bending/wrapping** the DNA. That torsional strain is transmitted to the adjacent AT-rich **13-mers**, whose weak (2-H-bond) A–T pairs melt → an **open complex** where helicase can load.

q: Why is the origin of replication AT-rich?
a: A–T pairs have only **2 hydrogen bonds** (vs 3 for G–C), so an AT-rich stretch takes **less energy to melt open** — ideal for the strand separation that starts replication.

q: Contrast the roles of DNA Pol III and DNA Pol I at the lagging strand.
a: **Pol III** rapidly synthesizes the bulk of each Okazaki fragment until it hits the next RNA primer, then leaves the gap. **Pol I** (high affinity for DNA–RNA gaps) removes the RNA primer (5′→3′ exonuclease) and fills DNA behind it; **ligase** then seals the final nick.

q: DON'T confuse the two Pol I exonuclease directions — what does each do?
a: **5′→3′ exonuclease** = removes the **RNA primer** ahead of the polymerase (Pol I only). **3′→5′ exonuclease** = **proofreading**, backing up to clip a just-added wrong nucleotide (Pol I *and* Pol III). Same enzyme family, opposite directions, different jobs.

q: How does a polymerase detect and fix a misincorporated nucleotide?
a: A mismatch can't hydrogen-bond properly, so the daughter strand's 3′ end is displaced from the "palm" polymerase site into the **3′→5′ exonuclease site**; the wrong nucleotide (plus a few) is clipped, the strand rotates back, and **5′→3′ synthesis resumes**.

q: Why can't a linear chromosome be replicated all the way to its end?
a: The lagging strand needs an RNA primer near the very end of its template; when that terminal primer is removed there's **no upstream 3′-OH** to fill the gap (a polymerase can't run the other way). So a short 3′ overhang goes unreplicated and the chromosome shortens each cycle.

q: How does telomerase extend the chromosome end?
a: Telomerase carries an **RNA template** complementary to the telomere repeat. It base-pairs to the **3′ G-rich overhang**, uses its RNA as template to **add DNA repeats (5′→3′)** to that overhang, then **translocates** and repeats — lengthening the overhang (it does NOT fill the 5′ gap).

q: After telomerase extends the single-stranded G overhang, how does that end become double-stranded again?
a: In the **next cell cycle**, the extended overhang serves as template and is filled in as part of the **lagging strand (Okazaki fragments)** — i.e., the complementary C-strand is made by normal lagging-strand synthesis.

q: Why does telomere shortening limit cell division, and what's it called?
a: Once telomeres get too short, further division risks losing real genes, so the cell stops dividing — **senescence** (lecture) / triggers **apoptosis** (reading). This division cap is the **Hayflick limit**, and it contributes to aging (damaged cells can't be replaced).

q: Why is "turn on telomerase to cure aging" dangerous?
a: Reactivating telomerase removes the Hayflick brake on division — the same thing **cancer** cells do (~80–90% reactivate telomerase). You'd trade aging for **cancer risk**: the aging↔cancer balance.

q: Why must PCR primers be oriented with their 3′ ends pointing *toward* each other, and what happens if they aren't? *(she flagged this as exam-important)*
a: Each primer's 3′-OH must point **into the target** so synthesis runs toward the other primer — that way each strand builds template for the other, giving **exponential** amplification. Primers pointing outward (or same direction) don't bracket the target → no exponential product.

q: How is PCR a "stripped-down" replication — what replaces the cell's machinery?
a: **Heat** replaces helicase/SSB (melts the strands); **synthetic DNA primers** replace primase/RNA primers (so no primer removal needed → one continuous DNA product); a **thermostable polymerase (Taq)** + dNTPs + buffer + Mg²⁺ do the synthesis.

q: When you read a Sanger sequencing gel, what sequence are you reading, and how does it relate to the template? *(she said this is likely on the exam)*
a: You read the **synthesized** strand — bottom-to-top of the gel = **5′→3′** (smallest fragment ran farthest = the earliest/5′-most terminated base). The **template** strand is its **complement** (antiparallel). Know which is which: the gel bands are the *new* strand, not the template.

q: Why does a dideoxynucleotide stop the chain, and how does using a small fraction of one ddNTP give a readable ladder?
a: No **3′-OH** on the ddNTP → no next phosphodiester bond → termination. Using mostly normal dNTPs + a **small %** of one ddNTP means the chain terminates **randomly** at each occurrence of that base → a **ladder** of fragments of every length ending in that base, which you separate by size.

q: Topoisomerase and DNA ligase both re-join DNA — how are their jobs different?
a: **Topoisomerase/gyrase** cut DNA to relieve **supercoiling** (torsional strain ahead of the fork), then reseal — a transient cut-rotate-rejoin. **Ligase** permanently seals the **nick between Okazaki fragments**. Topoisomerase is not the ligase enzyme, though it has re-joining ability.

q: In the Meselson–Stahl experiment (semiconservative), what is the ratio of **light : hybrid** DNA duplexes after the **4th** replication cycle?
worked: Semiconservative: the 2 original parental strands persist as **2 hybrid** duplexes every generation; total duplexes = 2ⁿ. After n cycles, light = 2ⁿ − 2, so light:hybrid = (2ⁿ−2)/2 = 2ⁿ⁻¹ − 1. Gen1 → 0 (all hybrid); Gen2 → 1:1; Gen3 → 3:1; **Gen4 → 2³ − 1 = 7 → 7:1.**
answer: 7 : 1 (light : hybrid).

q: For the semiconservative model, what does the **3rd generation** density gradient look like? (A) same as 2nd gen (B) the higher/light band gets thicker while the intermediate/hybrid band stays the same (C) the lower/hybrid band gets thicker while the light stays the same (D) two equal bands.
worked: Each cycle the hybrid amount is constant (2 duplexes) and the light-light amount grows. So gen 3 vs gen 2: the **light band thickens**, hybrid unchanged.
answer: B (light band thickens; hybrid band constant). *(Confirmed in lecture.)*

q: The *E. coli* chromosome (single origin, two forks moving outward until they meet) is 4×10⁶ bp and takes 40 min to copy. What is the speed of **one** replication fork in bp/min?
worked: Two forks split the circle, so each fork copies half: 4×10⁶ / 2 = 2×10⁶ bp. Over 40 min: 2×10⁶ / 40 = **5×10⁴**.
answer: 50,000 bp/min per fork.

q: The largest *Drosophila* chromosome is ~6×10⁷ bp and is replicated in <10 min. If it used a **single** origin at the center, what fork speed would the machinery need?
worked: Single central origin → 2 forks, each copies half: 6×10⁷ / 2 = 3×10⁷ bp in 10 min → 3×10⁷ / 10 = **3×10⁶**.
answer: 3,000,000 bp/min per fork (physically impossible — motivating multiple origins).

q: Same *Drosophila* chromosome (6×10⁷ bp), but real forks synthesize only **5,000 bp/min**. What is the minimum number of origins needed to finish in 10 min?
worked: Each origin fires **two** forks. In 10 min one fork does 5,000×10 = 50,000 bp, so one origin covers 2×50,000 = **100,000 bp**. Origins needed = 6×10⁷ / 1×10⁵ = **600**.
answer: 600 origins (minimum).

q: What enzymatic activity is present in telomerase? (1) DNA-dependent DNA polymerase (2) RNA-dependent DNA polymerase (3) DNA-dependent RNA polymerase (4) RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (5) none.
worked: Telomerase makes **DNA** using its own **RNA** template — a reverse transcriptase = RNA-dependent DNA polymerase.
answer: 2 — RNA-dependent DNA polymerase.

q: How is telomeric DNA converted from single-stranded (the extended overhang) to double-stranded form? (1) never, always ss (2) never, extended as ds (3) next cell cycle as part of the leading strand (4) next cell cycle as Okazaki fragments (5) two cell cycles later.
worked: The extended G-overhang is filled in during the next round as the complementary (C) strand — made by lagging-strand, i.e. **Okazaki-fragment**, synthesis.
answer: 4 — in the next cell cycle as Okazaki fragments.

q: A temperature-sensitive lethal *E. coli* dies just after replication begins at 42 °C. The DNA is composed of very long strands **plus** numerous ~1000-nt fragments. (a) Which enzyme is likely affected? (b) How were the small fragments formed?
worked: Long strands = leading strand (fine). Numerous unjoined ~1000-nt pieces = **Okazaki fragments** that got synthesized but never sealed → the joining enzyme is broken. Pol III (synthesis) and Pol I (primer removal) both still worked (fragments are full DNA), so the defect is in **DNA ligase**.
answer: (a) **DNA ligase**. (b) They are **Okazaki fragments** — the lagging strand is synthesized discontinuously as short RNA-primed pieces that normally get joined by ligase; here they remain separate.

q: In *E. coli* oriC, what happens if the ~100 bp **between** the 13-mer array and the 9-mer array is deleted? (1) DNA degradation (2) the 13-mer and 9-mer consensus sequences are sequenced as one (3) replication won't proceed — there isn't proper space/geometry to open the origin and load the replisome (4) the replisome can't find oriC.
worked: The 9-mers (DnaA boxes) are intact, so DnaA still **finds** oriC → (4) is wrong. No nuclease is invoked → (1) wrong; "sequenced as one" is a non-sequitur → (2) wrong. But DnaA at the 9-mers must be correctly **spaced/phased** relative to the 13-mers to bend the DNA and melt them; deleting the spacer disrupts open-complex formation, so initiation/replication won't proceed.
answer: 3 — replication will not proceed. DnaA still binds the intact 9-mers so it *finds* oriC (→ #4 is wrong), but deleting ~100 bp (~10 helical turns) between the boxes and the AT-rich 13-mers destroys the spacing/phasing DnaA needs to bend and melt them → no open complex → initiation fails. *(Mechanism-confirmed vs. the E. coli oriC literature — spacing here is "strictly critical." #3's "not enough space" wording is loose, but it's the only "won't proceed" option.)*

q: Which statement about **prokaryotic** replication is TRUE? (A) only two primers are needed (continuous leading strands both ways) (B) DNA ligase is dispensable because leading & lagging strands meet (C) no genetic material is lost because multiple bubbles eventually meet (D) telomeres are dispensable because the chromosomes are circular (E) SSBs aren't needed because bacterial DNA is single-stranded.
worked: A false (lagging strand needs many primers). B false (ligase still joins Okazaki fragments). C false (prokaryotes use a **single** origin, not multiple). E false (bacterial DNA is double-stranded; only some viruses are ss). **D true** — telomeres are only needed for **linear** ends; a circular chromosome has none.
answer: D. *(Confirmed in lecture.)*

q: You reactivate telomerase in adult human cells "to cure aging." What is the major danger? (best answer)
worked: Removing the Hayflick brake lets cells divide without limit — exactly what cancer cells exploit (telomerase reactivated in ~80–90% of cancers).
answer: Greatly increased **cancer** risk. *(Confirmed in lecture — answer C.)*

q: Why was Taq polymerase chosen for early PCR? (best answer)
worked: PCR's denaturation step hits ~95 °C, which destroys ordinary polymerases. Taq (from *Thermus aquaticus*) is **thermostable** and survives it — so you don't re-add enzyme each cycle. (Its lack of proofreading is a known downside, but heat-stability wins.)
answer: It is **thermostable / works near boiling.** *(Confirmed — answer B.)*

q: To PCR-amplify a region whose **top (coding) strand** is 5′-AGGCGG…-3′, the **forward** primer should match/relate to which strand, and roughly what should each primer's start look like?
worked: The **forward primer = same sequence as the top strand's 5′ region** (it binds the bottom strand and extends across the top), so it starts like the top strand: **5′-AGG…**. The **reverse primer = reverse complement of the top strand's 3′ region** (binds the top strand), so it starts **5′-GCC…**. Both 3′-OHs point inward.
answer: Forward = 5′ end of the top strand (starts AGG…); reverse = reverse complement of the other end (starts GCC…). *(Confirmed — answer D.)*

q: Starting from a single double-stranded target, how many copies of the target region exist after **25** PCR cycles (100% efficiency)?
worked: Each cycle doubles: after n cycles = 2ⁿ. 2²⁵ = **33,554,432** ≈ 3.4×10⁷.
answer: 2²⁵ ≈ 3.4×10⁷ (about 33.5 million).

q: For the duplex whose one strand is 5′-ACGACGCTA-3′: (a) give the complementary strand with polarity; (b) count total phosphodiester bonds and total hydrogen bonds in the duplex.
worked: (a) Complement (antiparallel): 3′-TGCTGCGAT-5′. (b) Each 9-nt strand has 8 phosphodiester bonds → 8×2 = **16**. Base pairs: sequence A,C,G,A,C,G,C,T,A → count A/T pairs = 4 (×2 H-bonds = 8), G/C pairs = 5 (×3 = 15) → **23** H-bonds.
answer: (a) 3′-TGCTGCGAT-5′; (b) 16 phosphodiester bonds, 23 hydrogen bonds.

q: A bacterial genome is ~15% adenine. What is the probability of a **specific 8-bp sequence** occurring at a given position, and about how many times would it appear in a 4.4×10⁶ bp genome? (assume Chargaff)
worked: 15% A → 15% T (A=T), so G+C = 70% → G = C = 35%. Each position's probability = that base's frequency (0.15 for A/T, 0.35 for G/C); an 8-mer's probability = the product of its 8 positions. Expected count ≈ P × genome length. (E.g., an all-GC 8-mer: 0.35⁸ ≈ 2.25×10⁻⁴ → ×4.4×10⁶ ≈ **~990 times**; an all-AT 8-mer: 0.15⁸ ≈ 2.56×10⁻⁷ → ~1 time.)
answer: P(8-mer) = product of the 8 base frequencies; expected count = P × 4.4×10⁶. (Plug in the actual primer-binding 8-mer's bases from the discussion sheet.)

q: You are studying an E. coli strain whose DNA Pol I has a point mutation that kills ONLY its 5'→3' exonuclease activity; its 3'→5' exonuclease and polymerase activities are intact. Which normal replication event fails, and which is preserved? Explain why the two exonucleases are not interchangeable.
a: FAILS: RNA-primer removal. The 5'→3' exonuclease is what chews out RNA-primer nucleotides one-by-one (starting at the primer's 5' nucleotide, pushing the gap 3'-ward) so the polymerase can backfill DNA. Without it, RNA primers stay in the daughter strand and Okazaki fragments can't be finished/ligated. PRESERVED: proofreading — the 3'→5' exonuclease still backs up to clip a just-added wrong nucleotide. Not interchangeable because they run in OPPOSITE directions doing OPPOSITE jobs: 5'→3' exo removes the primer AHEAD of the polymerase; 3'→5' exo proofreads BEHIND it. (Archbold's guaranteed trap: 5'→3' exo = primer removal, 3'→5' exo = proofreading.)

q: At E. coli oriC, DnaA binds the 9-mer boxes yet the strands actually melt apart over in the 13-mer region. Walk through the mechanism connecting those two facts, and explain why the 13-mers (not the 9-mers) are where the DNA opens.
a: DnaA binds the four 9-mers (DnaA boxes) and self-associates/oligomerizes, bending and wrapping the DNA around itself. That torsional strain is transmitted to the ADJACENT AT-rich 13-mer tandem repeats, whose weak A-T pairs (2 H-bonds each) melt open → an 'open complex'. The 13-mers open (not the 9-mers) because they're AT-rich, so they take the least energy to denature — the 9-mers are just the protein-docking/anchoring site that generates the strain. DnaC then loads DnaB (helicase) onto the exposed single strands.

q: You have a temperature-sensitive lethal E. coli that dies just after replication starts at 42 C. At the restrictive temperature it still makes DNA via Pol III and still removes RNA primers and replaces them with DNA — but its finished DNA is a mix of very long strands PLUS many unjoined ~1000-nt fragments. Which enzyme is defective, and what are the short fragments?
a: Defective enzyme = DNA ligase. Reasoning: the long strands = leading strand (fine); Pol III synthesis works and primer removal (Pol I) works — so the fragments are complete DNA, not RNA-primed hybrids. The only remaining step is sealing the nicks between adjacent fragments, which is ligase's sole job. The short pieces are Okazaki fragments: the lagging strand is built discontinuously as short primed segments that normally get joined by ligase — here they stay separate. (Contrast: if primer removal ALSO failed, suspect Pol I instead.)

topic: Pol I vs II vs III
q: Contrast the three principal bacterial DNA polymerases (I, II, III) by primary job and proofreading, and name the ONE exonuclease activity that sets Pol I apart from the other two.
a: Pol III = the workhorse, does the bulk of new DNA synthesis (highest processivity), proofreads 3'→5'. Pol I = removes RNA primers and fills the gaps, plus DNA repair, proofreads 3'→5'. Pol II = DNA repair, proofreads 3'→5'. All three proofread (3'→5' exonuclease). The activity UNIQUE to Pol I is the 5'→3' exonuclease — that's what lets only Pol I strip out RNA primers. (Roman numerals = prokaryotic; Greek letters = eukaryotic.)

q: Both topoisomerase/gyrase and DNA ligase cut-and-rejoin DNA backbone bonds — a classic Archbold 'important distinction.' What distinct problem does each solve, and why is calling topoisomerase 'a ligase' wrong?
a: Topoisomerase/gyrase relieves SUPERCOILING: unwinding the helix ahead of the fork torques the rest of a closed circle (like twisting a coiled phone cord); topoisomerase makes a TRANSIENT cut in one or both strands, lets the DNA swivel/untwist, then reseals the SAME break. DNA ligase makes a PERMANENT seal of the nick BETWEEN two separate Okazaki fragments, forming a new phosphodiester bond to join them. So topoisomerase has re-joining ability but is NOT the ligase enzyme — different substrate (its own transient cut vs. a fragment-to-fragment nick) and different purpose (torsional relief vs. joining pieces).

q: On its own DNA Pol III has low processivity, yet in vivo it stays on the template for tens of thousands of nucleotides. What is the sliding clamp, how does it physically achieve this, and what does 'processivity' mean?
a: Processivity = how far a polymerase stays bound and keeps synthesizing before falling off the template. The sliding clamp is a ring (~75 Å across, ~35 Å central hole) that ENCIRCLES the double-stranded DNA and physically tethers Pol III to the template, so the enzyme can't diffuse away — dramatically boosting processivity. When template runs out, the clamp drops Pol III and it's replaced by Pol I (for primer removal). (Distinct from the C-shaped polymerase 'hand' that only cups DNA on one side.)

q: When DNA Pol I replaces an RNA primer on the lagging strand, from WHICH end of the primer does it start, in which direction does the single-stranded gap travel, and why can't a polymerase instead just fill the gap by running the opposite way?
a: Pol I starts at the 5'-most nucleotide of the RNA primer and works 5'→3', so the single-stranded gap is pushed in the 3' direction as each RNA nucleotide is excised and backfilled with DNA. A polymerase can't 'run the other way' to fill a gap because ALL polymerases synthesize only 5'→3' onto a free 3'-OH ('follow the 3' hydroxyl') — there's no 3'→5' synthesis, so filling must proceed from an upstream 3'-OH toward the gap, not backward into it. (This same constraint is exactly why linear chromosome ends can't be filled — the end-replication problem.)

q: By what activity does a DNA polymerase proofread, what physically triggers the correction at the enzyme, and how accurate is wild-type E. coli replication as a result?
a: Proofreading uses the 3'→5' exonuclease activity. Trigger: a misincorporated base can't hydrogen-bond properly, so the daughter strand's 3'-OH end is displaced out of the 'palm' polymerase site and rotates into the 3'→5' exonuclease site (at the 'heel' of the hand); the wrong nucleotide (plus a few neighbors) is clipped, the strand rotates back, and 5'→3' synthesis resumes. Net error rate ≈ 1 in 10^9 nucleotides. (It's a brief 'rewind,' NOT a change of synthesis direction.)

q: On a linear chromosome, exactly why does the very end of the lagging-strand template go unreplicated when its terminal RNA primer is removed?
a: Removing the terminal RNA primer leaves a gap, and there is no upstream 3'-OH for a polymerase to extend into it (Pol I can only fill 5'→3' from an adjacent primer's 3'-OH, and no polymerase runs the other way). So a short 3' overhang stays single-stranded and the 5' end is not completed — the chromosome shortens each cycle.

q: Archbold called telomere loss two problems and said the more urgent one is the one the textbook underplays. Beyond chromosomes shrinking over generations, what is the immediate danger of a bare 3' overhang, and what would happen to a human cell without telomere capping?
a: A bare single-stranded overhang looks like a double-strand break, so double-strand-break repair machinery would fuse chromosome ends together. In humans that means the 46 chromosomes get glued into one giant chromosome with 46 centromeres, which is torn apart at mitosis/meiosis → the cell dies. The gradual gene loss is the 'tomorrow' problem; the DSB-mimic/end-fusion is the worse 'today' problem.

q: What type of enzyme is telomerase, what two components does it carry, which strand/end does it act on, and which direction does it build?
a: A ribonucleoprotein reverse transcriptase = an RNA-dependent DNA polymerase (protein + its own RNA template), encoded by TERT. It base-pairs its C-rich RNA template to the G-rich 3' overhang (the 'G-strand') and adds DNA telomere repeats onto that 3' end, building 5'→3' like every other polymerase.

q: Archbold told you to cross out the telomere figure in the textbook. What does that figure get wrong about what telomerase does, what does telomerase actually accomplish, and how does Shelterin finish the job?
a: The book draws the overhang getting filled in to make a blunt double-stranded end — it never really does. Telomerase does NOT fill the 5' gap; it makes the 3' G-overhang LONGER (long enough to grab). Shelterin proteins then fold that end into a T-loop (with a D-loop where the overhang tucks back and base-pairs upstream), capping it like an aglet so DSB-repair proteins can't see it and fuse the ends. It's 'hidden, not filled.'

q: Once telomeres get too short, why does the cell stop dividing, what is that state called (lecture vs reading), and why is 'switch telomerase back on to cure aging' a dangerous idea?
a: Further division would start deleting real genes, so the cell permanently stops dividing — senescence (lecture framing; the reading emphasizes telomere-triggered apoptosis instead — know both). This division cap is the Hayflick limit and contributes to aging because worn-out cells can't be replaced. Reactivating telomerase removes that brake — which is exactly what cancer does (telomerase reactivated in ~80-90% of cancers), so you'd trade aging for cancer risk: the aging↔cancer balance.

q: PCR is replication with the cellular machinery stripped out. For each of these replication jobs, name what PCR uses instead: (a) unwinding the strands (helicase/SSB), (b) providing a starting 3'-OH (primase/RNA primer), (c) synthesis. And why does swapping in synthetic DNA primers simplify the product versus a cell?
a: (a) Heat (~95 °C denaturation) melts the strands apart — no helicase/SSB needed. (b) Synthetic DNA primers anneal directly — no primase and no RNA. (c) A thermostable polymerase (Taq, from Thermus aquaticus) + dNTPs + buffer + Mg2+ do the synthesis. Because the primers are DNA (not RNA), there is nothing to remove and replace afterward, so you get one continuous DNA product instead of primed Okazaki-style pieces.

src: transcript EXAM SIGNALS + §4.2 (htz1-style primer problem, clicker D)
q: You are amplifying a gene whose top (coding) strand reads 5'-ATGCCAGT......ACGTTCAG-3'. Give the sequence (5'→3') of the forward primer and the reverse primer that would amplify this region, and state the one rule about their 3' ends that makes amplification exponential. (She flagged primer directionality as exam-important.)
worked: Rule: the two primers' 3'-OH ends must point TOWARD each other (into the target) so each primer's extension builds template for the other → exponential amplification. Forward primer = same sequence as the 5' end of the top strand (it binds the bottom strand and extends across the top), so it copies the top's left end: 5'-ATGCCAGT-3'. Reverse primer = reverse complement of the top strand's 3' (right) end: top right = 5'-ACGTTCAG-3'; its complement is 3'-TGCAAGTC-5', written 5'→3' the reverse primer is 5'-CTGAACGT-3' (it binds the top strand and extends leftward). Both 3' ends now face inward.
answer: Forward primer: 5'-ATGCCAGT-3' (matches the top strand's 5' end). Reverse primer: 5'-CTGAACGT-3' (reverse complement of the top strand's 3' end). Key rule: the primers' 3'-OH ends must point toward each other; reversed/outward primers give no exponential product.

q: You start a PCR with 5 copies of a double-stranded target and run it at 100% efficiency. Give the general formula for target copies after n cycles, then compute the number of target-region copies after 20 cycles.
worked: Each cycle doubles every target molecule, so copies = (starting copies) × 2^n, NOT just 2^n (the 2^n formula assumes you began with a single molecule). Here starting = 5, n = 20. 2^20 = 1,048,576. Multiply: 5 × 1,048,576 = 5,242,880.
answer: Copies = (initial molecules) × 2^n. After 20 cycles: 5 × 2^20 = 5 × 1,048,576 = 5,242,880 (≈ 5.24 × 10^6).

topic: Read a Sanger gel → synthesized strand
src: transcript Lec2T §4.3 EXAM SIGNAL (read the gel; synth-vs-template) + reading 7.5
q: You sequence a fragment of the fictional pathogen *Mortis vulgaris* by classic Sanger sequencing, running four lanes (ddA, ddC, ddG, ddT) on a slab gel and exposing film. Reading from the BOTTOM of the gel (fastest-migrating band) up to the top, the bands fall in these lanes: A, C, G, G, T, A, C, T. Write the sequence of the SYNTHESIZED (new) strand with correct polarity.
worked: The bands are the newly synthesized strand, not the template. The smallest fragment migrates farthest, so it sits at the bottom; that fragment was terminated by the FIRST (5'-most) ddNTP added onto the primer. So reading the gel bottom→top gives the synthesized strand 5'→3' directly. The lane each band sits in names the terminating base = that position's base. Bottom→top (A C G G T A C T) is therefore the synthesized strand read 5'→3'.
answer: Synthesized strand: 5'-ACGGTACT-3' (gel read bottom→top = 5'→3').

src: transcript Lec2T §4.3 + EXAM SIGNAL (synthesized vs template) + reading 7.5
q: Same *Mortis vulgaris* gel. You have already read the synthesized strand off the gel as 5'-TCGGATCA-3'. The exam then asks for the sequence of the TEMPLATE strand that was actually being sequenced. Give it with polarity. (This is the synthesized-vs-template distinction she flagged.)
worked: The gel bands = the synthesized strand; the template is its complement AND antiparallel. Align them: synth 5'-TCGGATCA-3' pairs base-for-base with template written 3'-AGCCTAGT-5' beneath it (T·A, C·G, G·C, G·C, A·T, T·A, C·G, A·T). Rewriting that template in the conventional 5'→3' direction reverses it to 5'-TGATCCGA-3'. Do NOT just report the gel sequence as the template — that is the classic trap.
answer: Template strand: 3'-AGCCTAGT-5' (i.e. 5'-TGATCCGA-3'). It is the antiparallel complement of the synthesized strand, not the gel sequence itself.

q: You Sanger-sequence a strand whose TEMPLATE reads 3'-TACCGATG-5' (the primer binds just off its 3' end). Predict the order of bands you will see on the gel from BOTTOM (fastest) to TOP, i.e. list the terminating base at each rung of the ladder in order.
worked: Polymerase copies the template antiparallel and 5'→3', so the synthesized strand is the complement read left-to-right as the template is written 3'→5': template 3'-T A C C G A T G-5' gives synth 5'-A T G G C T A C-3' (T→A, A→T, C→G, C→G, G→C, A→T, T→A, G→C). The synthesized strand's 5'-most base is on the shortest fragment (bottom band); each next base adds one rung going up. So bottom→top the terminating bases are A, T, G, G, C, T, A, C.
answer: Bottom→top band order: A, T, G, G, C, T, A, C (= synthesized strand 5'-ATGGCTAC-3'). Bottom band = A (first/5'-most base added), top band = C.

q: A modern automated Sanger run uses four fluorescently labeled ddNTPs in ONE tube and separates products by capillary electrophoresis past a detector. The colored peaks come off the detector in this order: green, blue, blue, red, black, green. Given the color key A=green, C=blue, G=black, T=red, what is the synthesized sequence and its polarity, and how does elution order relate to fragment size?
worked: In capillary electrophoresis the SMALLEST fragment reaches the detector first, so the first peak corresponds to the 5'-most (first-added) base of the synthesized strand; peaks then appear in order of increasing fragment length. So the elution order directly spells the synthesized strand 5'→3'. Decode by color: green=A, blue=C, blue=C, red=T, black=G, green=A → A C C T G A, read 5'→3'.
answer: Synthesized strand: 5'-ACCTGA-3'. Smallest fragment elutes first, so peak order = synthesized strand 5'→3' (the template would be its antiparallel complement, 5'-TCAGGT-3').

src: reading 7.5 (small fraction ddNTP → ladder) + transcript Lec2T §4.3
q: In one Sanger tube you want a readable ladder for a template that directs synthesis of the 7-nt sequence 5'-GATTACA-3'. Explain why the reaction uses mostly normal dNTPs plus only a SMALL fraction of ddNTPs, and state how many distinct fragment LENGTHS this template can generate across the ladder.
worked: If every nucleotide were a ddNTP, synthesis would terminate at the very first base and you'd get only one product — no sequence information. Using mostly dNTPs with a small percentage of ddNTPs makes termination RANDOM: at each position, most strands extend but a minority incorporate a ddNTP and stop. Across the whole reaction this yields a population of fragments terminated at every position — one of each length. For a 7-nt synthesized stretch, termination can occur after base 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7, giving 7 distinct fragment lengths, each ending in the ddNTP matching that position (G, A, T, T, A, C, A).
answer: Mostly dNTPs + a small % ddNTPs makes termination random, so strands stop at every position → a complete ladder rather than one truncated product. A 7-nt read gives 7 distinct fragment lengths (terminating in G, A, T, T, A, C, A from shortest to longest).

src: transcript Lec2T §4.4 (Illumina reversible chain termination, restore 3'-OH)
q: High-throughput 'sequencing-by-synthesis' (Illumina) also uses chain terminators, yet unlike Sanger it reads millions of fragments and needs no size-separated gel ladder. What single chemical difference in its terminators makes this possible, and what extra step per cycle does it require?
worked: Sanger uses ddNTPs that are IRREVERSIBLY terminated (permanent loss of the 3'-OH), so each strand stops for good and you must sort a population of different-length fragments by size to read the order. Illumina instead uses REVERSIBLE terminators: each incoming base is blocked at the 3' position AND carries a removable fluorescent tag, so exactly one base is added, imaged for its color, then the block and dye are chemically cleaved to REGENERATE the 3'-OH — allowing the next base to add. Because you read the color at each cycle in place, you sequence one base at a time on each cluster without ever running a size ladder, and you do this across millions of fragments in parallel, then computationally overlap the reads into contigs.
answer: Reversible (not permanent) terminators: each base is 3'-blocked + dye-labeled, added one at a time, imaged, then chemically unblocked to restore the 3'-OH for the next base. The extra per-cycle step is that deblock/dye-cleavage. This replaces Sanger's size-sorted ladder with in-place, massively parallel base-by-base reads.
